Q1: I tried to keep a pretty structured routine, honestly. I'd set aside about four to five hours a day for studying, usually in the late afternoon after my other commitments. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time going through written materials, taking notes, and rewriting key concepts. It wasn't always perfect, but having that routine helped me manage my anxiety about keeping up with everything.
Q2: I aimed for regularity, but I'd be lying if I said it was always consistent. When deadlines piled up, I'd definitely shift into a more intense mode and study longer hours. The anxiety would spike during those weeks, and I'd find myself working late into the night. But during calmer periods, I stuck to my afternoon schedule pretty faithfully.
Q3: Usually it was to check if there were new assignments posted or to see announcements from instructors. I'd also log in when I needed to access reading materials or check discussion threads. Sometimes the peer support group I was part of would mention something happening on the platform, and that would prompt me to check in too.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks, logging in became almost automatic. I'd do it first thing when I sat down to study, just to see what was new. The peer support intervention actually helped with this because my study partners and I would coordinate through the platform. It became part of my daily rhythm, even if I didn't always spend a lot of time actively browsing once I was there.
Q5: The written course materials and lecture notes were essential for me since that's how I learn best. I also found the discussion forums helpful, not necessarily for posting myself, but for reading other students' questions and the instructor responses. It helped clarify things I was confused about without having to ask directly, which honestly reduced some of my anxiety.
Q6: I didn't watch many of the videos, to be honest. About 50 minutes total, which I know is low. I just retain information better when I read it, so I'd often skip videos and go straight to the transcripts or supplementary readings if they were available. It felt like a time trade-off, and reading was more efficient for me.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to the assignments or exams. If I knew the material would show up later, I was all in. Also, anything that helped reduce my uncertainty about what was expected felt valuable. Clear instructions and practical examples made me feel like my time was being spent wisely.
Q8: Definitely different. Required activities had this weight to them, like I couldn't relax until they were done. Optional ones felt more like a luxury I couldn't always afford, especially when stress was high. I'd tell myself I'd come back to them, but often I didn't. There was always something mandatory demanding my attention first.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded most of the PDFs and readings early in the semester and studied from those offline. I also kept a physical notebook where I'd rewrite important concepts by hand. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the screen, just me and my notes at my desk or sometimes at a café.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or working through practice problems I'd printed out. The LMS wouldn't capture any of that. Same with study sessions with my peer group where we'd meet in person and work through material together. That engagement was real but invisible online.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a reading, maybe skim it online first, then close my laptop and really dig into it later with a highlighter and pen. My best studying happened offline. The initial access was just me grabbing what I needed for later. The LMS saw a quick download, but I spent hours with that material afterward.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. I had multiple deadlines converging, and my anxiety was through the roof. I felt like I was just going through the motions, checking boxes rather than actually absorbing anything. There were a few weeks where I dreaded even opening my laptop because I knew how much was waiting for me.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS. I'd submit assignments but skip the optional discussions or additional readings. Competing responsibilities, like my extracurriculars and part-time commitments, sometimes meant I had to triage. The platform got less of my attention when life got hectic.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or watch part of a video without really taking anything in. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other deadlines or just feeling burnt out. I'd technically be logged in and active, but mentally I was checked out. It's frustrating when you realize you've wasted time like that.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts more than I should have. Even though I knew participating was good for my understanding, the anxiety of putting my thoughts out there publicly made me procrastinate. I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I had something smarter to say, and sometimes later never came.
Q16: Deadlines were my main driver, honestly. My LMS activity would spike right before something was due. I'd be calm for a while, then suddenly very active in the days leading up to a submission. It wasn't ideal for my stress levels, but that external pressure was what got me moving most of the time.
Q17: Feedback had a big impact on me. Positive feedback motivated me to keep going, while critical feedback, even when constructive, would sometimes make me anxious about the next assignment. I'd spend extra time on subsequent tasks trying to address whatever was pointed out. It shaped how carefully I approached things.
Q18: The course structure was pretty clear, which helped. Having a predictable schedule of when materials were released and when things were due allowed me to plan ahead. But when too many things clustered together, even a good structure couldn't save me from feeling overwhelmed. Spacing would have helped more.
Q19: The clear written instructions made engagement easier for someone like me who prefers reading. But the heavy reliance on video content was harder since that's not my strength. Also, when discussion requirements felt forced rather than organic, it was harder to engage authentically. I participated, but it sometimes felt performative.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for something, trying to understand what's expected or feeling lost about where to focus. They could also be anxious like me, checking and rechecking things without actually producing work. Sometimes high activity is a sign of confusion rather than engagement.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My LMS engagement was low, but I did okay because so much of my real studying happened offline. If someone's efficient with their time and knows how to learn independently, they don't need to be constantly logged in. The platform is a tool, not the whole picture.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns, like when you're active and what you access. But they miss the quality of that engagement and everything that happens offline. They can't measure understanding, anxiety, or how much you're actually learning. They're a partial snapshot at best.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, asking questions in my head, making connections to other things I know. It's more than logging in or submitting on time. It's an internal state that doesn't always show externally.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels or how much peer support helped me get through tough weeks. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with physical notes or the conversations with classmates that clarified difficult concepts. My emotional journey through the course, the stress and the small victories, none of that appears in the data.
